coredata.dc:title: Preparation of fluorescent organic nanoparticles from polyethylenimine and sucrose for cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Chunping
Zhang, Xiqi
Yang, Liutao
Wu, Yujiao
Liu, Hongliang
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: An approach for the synthesis of fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) with a quantum yield of about 14% has been developed using polyethylenimine and sucrose. The FONs were prepared under mild reaction condition. The obtained FONs showed high water dispersibility, intense fluorescence, excitation-dependent emission feature, excellent nanoparticle stability, and high photostability. The cytotoxicity of the FONs was also evaluated using A549 cells, and the cell viability value was demonstrated greater than 90% even when the concentration was up to 120μgmL−1, which proved excellent biocompatibility and made them promising for cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyethylenimine
Sucrose
Fluorescent organic nanoparticles
Biocompatibility
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells incubated with PEI-Suc FONs
Cell imaging applications of PEI-Suc FONs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of PEI-Suc FONs dispersed in water, scale bar=100nm. (B) Size distributions of PEI-Suc FONs.
(A) FT-IR spectra of PEI, sucrose, and PEI-Suc.

XPS spectra of PEI-Suc FONs: (A) Wide range XPS spectrum showing the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen; (B) high-resolution C 1s spectra of the PEI-Suc FONs; (C) high-resolution N 1s spectra of the PEI-Suc FONs; (D) high-resolution O 1s spectra of the PEI-Suc FONs.

(A) UV absorption spectrum of PEI-Suc FONs dispersed in water, inset is the visible image of PEI-Suc FONs in water; (B) PL emission spectra (right) of PEI-Suc FONs dispersed in water under different excitation wavelengths and the excitation spectrum (left), inset is the fluorescent images of PEI-Suc FONs taken under 365nm UV light.
(A) The change of fluorescence intensity of PEI-Suc FONs at different storage time. (B) The change of fluorescence intensity of PEI-Suc FONs at different illumination time under 365nm UV light.
Cell viability of A549 cells incubated with different concentrations of PEI-Suc FONs for 8h and 24h.

CLSM images of A549 cells incubated with 10μgmL−1 of PEI-Suc FONs for 3h. (A) bright field, (B) excited with 405nm laser, (C) excited with 458nm laser, and (D) merged image of A and B. Scale bar=20μm.
Preparation of PEI-Suc FONs from PEI and sucrose for cell imaging applications.

head.ce:title: Preparation of fluorescent organic nanoparticles from polyethylenimine and sucrose for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials & Metallurgical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang 550003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Bio-Inspired Smart Interface Science, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Bio-Inspired Smart Interface Science
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An approach for the synthesis of fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) with a quantum yield of about 14% has been developed using polyethylenimine and sucrose. The FONs were prepared under mild reaction condition. The obtained FONs showed high water dispersibility, intense fluorescence, excitation-dependent emission feature, excellent nanoparticle stability, and high photostability. The cytotoxicity of the FONs was also evaluated using A549 cells, and the cell viability value was demonstrated greater than 90% even when the concentration was up to 120μgmL−1, which proved excellent biocompatibility and made them promising for cell imaging.

Polyethylenimine (PEI) and sucrose were incorporated to construct fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) at mild condition, and further utilized for cell imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) have generated extensive attention for cellular fluorescence imaging owing to their good biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and flexible synthetic approaches [1]. However, the organic dyes that form the FONs are prone to aggregate in water due to the poor miscibility, limiting their biological applications [2]. Moreover, aggregation of the organic dyes usually leads to fluorescence quenching as a result of strong intermolecular π–π interactions among the organic aromatic ring [3]. Another problem for traditional organic dyes is the photobleaching, also hampering their application as bioprobes [4]. Recently, aggregation induced emission (AIE) dyes have been introduced into the FONs. These AIE FONs have exhibited high quantum yield without fluorescence quenching, and rapidly aroused great interest of many researchers [5–7]. However, the preparation of AIE FONs requires complex synthesized method and expensive reactant, limiting their large-scale synthesis. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), with low toxicity and good biocompatibility, have created immense attention in recent years and been widely used for bioimaging [8–10]. Various strategies for the preparation of fluorescent CDs have been reported, such as carbonizing organics, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation and hydrothermal methods [11]. And some green synthetic approaches for the preparation of CDs have also been developed by using inexpensive renewable resources as precursors like honey [12], ginger [13], lychee seeds [14], and peanut shells [15]. However, most of these approaches suffer from either high reaction temperatures or long preparation times. In this case, preparation of fluorescent nanoparticles without complex synthesis, expensive reactant, high reaction temperatures, and long preparation times is highly demanded.

Polyethylenimine (PEI) is a polycationic polymer with repeating unit of amine and ethyl group. The highly reactive amino groups can react with epoxy, aldehyde, and isocyanate compound, and find many applications in formaldehyde adsorbent, dye fixing agent, and epoxy resin reaction agent. PEI is still a polymeric transfection agent and extremely attractive for building siRNA carriers [16–18]. Sucrose is a renewable, naturally occurring carbohydrate found in cane or beet sugar. The molecule is a disaccharide combination of the monosaccharides glucose and fructose, and hydrolysis will break the glycosidic bond converting sucrose into glucose and fructose.

In this study, a strategy of preparing FONs was developed for the first time via combining PEI and sucrose at mild condition (Scheme 1 ). The obtained FONs showed intense fluorescence, high water dispersibility, and excellent stability. The photostability and cytotoxicity of the FONs were evaluated, and their cell imaging application has also been investigated.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, M n =600) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, sucrose was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. All other solvents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

A mixture of PEI (100mg) and sucrose (200mg) was dispersed in 20mL deionized water, and then heated at 100°C for 2h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was purified by dialysis through porous cellulose bag (molecular weight cut-off: 3500Da) using ethanol. Finally, the PEI-Suc FONs inside the dialysis bag was collected and dried by vacuum oven at 40°C.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained on a HT7700 microscope (Hitachi, Japan) operated at 100kV, the TEM samples in solution phase were drop-cast onto a carbon-coated copper grid, and the grid was air dried at room temperature (25°C) before loading into the microscope. Size distributions of PEI-Suc FONs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was measured using a zeta Plus apparatus (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out on a VGESCALAB 220-IXL spectrometer equipped with an Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6eV). The energy scale was internally calibrated by referencing to the binding energy (Eb) of the C1s peak of a carbon contaminant at 284.6eV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were conducted in a transmission mode on a Shimadzu Spectrum 8400 spectrometer (Japan). Typically, 8 scans at a resolution of 1cm−1 were accumulated to obtain one spectrum. For XPS and FT-IR measurement, the fluorescence dye was characterized in dried state. UV–visible absorption spectrum was recorded on UV/Vis/NIR 2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using quartz cuvettes of 1cm path length. Fluorescence spectra were measured on an F-4600 spectrometer with a slit width of 3nm for both excitation and emission.

The cell viability of PEI-Suc FONs on A549 cells was evaluated by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 5×104 cells mL−1 in 160μL of respective media containing 10% FBS. After 24h of cell attachment, the cells were incubated with 10, 20, 40, 80, 120μgmL−1 PEI-Suc FONs for 8 and 24h. Then nanoparticles were removed and cells were washed with PBS three times. 10μL of CCK-8 dye and 100μL of DMEM cell culture medium were added to each well and incubated for 2h at 37°C. Plates were then analyzed with a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of formazan dye absorbance were carried out at 450nm, with the reference wavelength at 620nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 dye was compared to controls (cells not exposure to PEI-Suc FONs), which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used per microplate, and the experiment was repeated three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells incubated with PEI-Suc FONs
ce:section.ce:para: A549 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, 2mM glutamine, 100UmL−1 penicillin, and 100μgmL−1 of streptomycin. Cell culture was maintained at 37°C in a humidified condition of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium. Culture medium was changed every three days for maintaining the exponential growth of the cells. On the day prior to treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with PEI-Suc FONs at a final concentration of 10μgmL−1 for 3h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the PEI-Suc FONs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature. Cell images were taken with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zesis 710 3-channel (Zesis, Germany) with the excitation wavelength of 405nm and 458nm.

For the characterization of the morphology, chemical composition, and fluorescent properties of the PEI-Suc FONs, the following techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–visible absorption spectrum, and fluorescence spectra were investigated to determine the properties of PEI-Suc FONs.

The morphology and structure of the PEI-Suc FONs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1A). The prepared FONs are well dispersed and exhibited a spherical morphology with diameters ranging from 10 to 20nm. The TEM images provided direct evidence of the formation of nanoparticles in aqueous solution. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 1B, the size distribution of PEI-Suc FONs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was conducted on a zeta Plus particle size analyzer, indicating the size distribution at the range of 5–29nm, with an average size of 17nm. The size distribution of the FONs from DLS was in consistent with the TEM result.

The functional groups on the PEI-Suc FONs were investigated using FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 , in the sample of PEI, the characteristic peak located at 3275cm−1 was assigned to the NH2 group, another two peaks at 2936 and 2817cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the CH2 groups. Moreover, the peaks at 1577cm−1 and 1064cm−1 assigned to bending vibration of -NH2 and stretching vibration of the CN bond were also found in PEI. For the sample of sucrose, the broad peak of OH located at 3328cm−1 could be corresponded to the stretching vibration of the –OH group, while strong stretching vibration of the CO band was found at 1040cm−1. After reaction of PEI and sucrose at 100°C for 2h to obtain the PEI-Suc FONs, several characteristic peaks could be clearly observed. The signal of NH2 and OH groups was found at 3275cm−1, and the CH2 group located at 2982 and 2825cm−1 was also observed, which was corresponded to that of PEI. Strong stretching vibrations of the CO bond found at 1041cm−1 and the CN bond located at 1084cm−1 also emerged in the FONs. More importantly, the stretching vibration of the CN band located at 1630cm−1 and bending vibration of CH group located at 808cm−1 could be clearly identified, demonstrating the reaction possibly involved hydrolysis of sucrose and formation of Schiff base between PEI and glucose.

The XPS spectra were also conducted to analysis the surface state and composition of the PEI-Suc FONs. As shown in Fig. 3A, three peaks at 285, 400 and 532eV exist in the XPS spectra, indicating the presence of C, N and O elements in the PEI-Suc FONs, respectively. The overall wt% of elements present in the FONs was calculated as C:N:O~66:13:21. The high resolution spectrum of C1s exhibits three main peaks located at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.6eV, which are attributed to CC, CO and CN, respectively (Fig. 3B). This result proved the formation of CN bond in the Schiff base resulted from the condensation of PEI and glucose, as the hydrolysis of sucrose afforded the glucose, which was also confirmed by the FT-IR spectra. The high resolution spectrum of N 1s spectrum showed peak centered at 399.5eV (Fig. 3C), while the high resolution spectrum of O 1s spectrum showed peaks at 531.8eV (Fig. 3D). All these results supported that PEI and sucrose successfully generated the PEI-Suc FONs.

The inset of Fig. 4A showed that PEI-Suc FONs were readily dispersed in water with high transparency. Then, the UV absorption spectrum of the as-prepared PEI-Suc FONs was investigated (Fig. 4A). The absorption spectrum showed a wide absorption band without any obvious characteristic peak, possibly due to the relatively broad size distribution of the FONs. Meanwhile, it is found that the absorption spectrum started to increase from 750nm, indicating the existence of nanoparticles in the solution caused by the Mie effect [19]. The PL spectra of the PEI-Suc FONs were shown in Fig. 4B, which showed obvious excitation-dependent feature. The emission peaks shifted from 460 to 524nm with an increase in the excitation wavelength ranging from 320 to 480nm, which covered the wavelength ranging from blue to green. Moreover, the excitation-dependent feature of FONs has also been demonstrated by some other types of fluorescent nanoparticles [20], however, the mechanism is still unclear. The inset of Fig. 4B showed the fluorescent image of PEI-Suc FONs taken under UV light of 365nm, exhibiting bright blue fluorescence. Using 9,10-diphenylanthracene as the reference, the fluorescence quantum yield was estimated to be about 14% at an excitation wavelength of 365nm. This intense fluorescence suggested that the as-prepared FONs were suitable for the potential cell imaging applications. It should be noted that PEI or sucrose alone did not show fluorescent emission, respectively, however, PEI-Suc FONs showed fluorescent emission, which might be attributed to the hydrolysis of sucrose and formation of Schiff base between PEI and glucose, as the CN group was evidenced in the FONs by FT-IR and XPS experiment. And the formation of CN bond would enhance the fluorescence efficiency significantly, which has been reported in the previous literature [21].

The PEI-Suc FONs in aqueous solution exhibited a long-term homogeneous phase without any apparent precipitation at room temperature. In addition, the fluorescence intensity has no significant change during long-term storage for 8weeks (Fig. 5A). Furthermore, no obvious attenuation of the fluorescence intensity was observed under continuous UV irradiation of 365nm for 90min (Fig. 5B). The above results demonstrated these PEI-Suc FONs had excellent nanoparticle stability and high photostability, which is beneficial for cell imaging application.

The biocompatibility was conducted to evaluate the potential biomedical applications of PEI-Suc FONs. The effect of PEI-Suc FONs to A549 cells (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells) was investigated after the cells were incubated with different concentrations of the FONs, and cell viability of PEI-Suc FONs to A549 cells was determined by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. As shown in Fig. 6 , when the cells were incubated with 10–120μgmL−1 of PEI-Suc FONs for 8h and 24h, respectively, no obvious decrease of cell viability was found. Even when the concentration was raised up to 120μgmL−1, the cell viability value was still greater than 90%, indicating the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of the FONs, which can be used as a safe probe for in vivo applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging applications of PEI-Suc FONs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the merits of the PEI-Suc FONs, including high water dispersibility, intense fluorescence, excitation-dependent emission feature, excellent nanoparticle stability, high photostability, and excellent biocompatibility, the FONs were applied for multicolor living cell imaging. Cell imaging of the PEI-Suc FONs was explored, and the in vitro cellular uptake of PEI-Suc FONs was evaluated by Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) observation. As shown in Fig. 7B, when excited under laser of 405nm, strong blue fluorescence could be observed in the cytoplasm region after only incubated with 10μgmL−1 of PEI-Suc FONs. When change the excitation wavelength of the laser to 458nm, bright green fluorescence could be seen in the same region (Fig. 7C). It is found that PEI-Suc FONs could be uptaken by cells and mainly located in the cytoplasm and could not enter into the cell nuclei. The reason might be due to no specific cell tagging groups in the FONs, and the FONs were mainly internalized by cells through the endocytosis pathway [22]. This result indicated that the PEI-Suc FONs internalized in the cell could emit multicolor fluorescence under a laser of different wavelengths, and the excitation-dependent emission characteristic of the FONs enables them multicolor cell imaging, which has also been reported in the previous literatures [21,23]. Owing to the intense fluorescence derived from the PEI-Suc FONs, strong fluorescent signal could be detected, which was highly beneficial for cell imaging.

In summary, a strategy of preparing FONs was developed for the first time via combining PEI and sucrose at mild condition. The reaction was conducted at 100°C for 2h to afford PEI-Suc FONs. The prepared FONs are well dispersed in water, and exhibited a spherical morphology according to the TEM result, and with an average size of 17nm obtained from the DLS result. The FT-IR spectra and XPS results proposed the mechanism of the formation of PEI-Suc FONs might involve hydrolysis of sucrose and formation of Schiff base between PEI and glucose. The PEI-Suc FONs showed intense fluorescence with a quantum yield of about 14%, and the fluorescent emission of the FONs represented obvious excitation-dependent feature. We also demonstrated these PEI-Suc FONs had excellent nanoparticle stability and high photostability. Biocompatibility evaluation and cell imaging application suggested that these FONs were successfully applied towards multicolor living cell imaging. More importantly, the preparation strategy in this work can extend to other water soluble polymers with amino groups and various carbohydrates, which would provide a new way to prepare more biocompatible fluorescent materials for biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Guizhou Province Science and Technology Planning Project ([2015]2068), the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21134004, 21474057 and 51503046), the National 973 Project (No. 2011CB935700), the Research Project for High-level Talents of Guizhou Institute of Technology (XJGC201503), and the Scientific & Technological Innovation Talent Team of Guizhou Province (Qian Ke He Talent Team [2015]4008).
